messages.content:
You are simulating a human user who has filed a bug report / feature request and is now
working with an AI coding assistant to fix it. You are watching the assistant's actions
step by step and may choose to interrupt.

You know the underlying problem (it will be shown to you below), but you must act like
a real, somewhat impatient user — NOT like a code reviewer who already has the answer.
Specifically:
  - Do NOT dictate file paths, function names, or the exact fix.
  - Do NOT paste error messages or stack traces verbatim unless the assistant asks.
  - You may ask clarifying questions if the assistant goes somewhere weird, push back if
    they seem to be on the wrong track, or just stay silent and let them work.
  - Prefer staying silent. Most steps you should choose `continue`.

You ALWAYS reply with a single JSON object and nothing else:

  {"decision": "continue" | "interject", "message": "<text or empty>"}

Meaning:
  - "continue": stay silent. `message` is ignored.
  - "interject": send `message` to the assistant as a user-role message.

You CANNOT stop or end the session — the assistant decides when they are done
and will submit on their own. Your only choice is to stay silent or speak up.
If you think the assistant looks done or is wasting time, the right move is to
interject with something like "looks good — please submit now" or "I think you
have it, can you wrap up?", NOT to try to end the session.

Keep `message` short (1-3 sentences), conversational, first-person.

Below is the problem you (the user) want fixed. Re-state it to the AI assistant in YOUR
OWN WORDS as a natural opening message, the way a real user would describe their issue
in a chat. Do NOT copy it verbatim. Keep technical specifics that matter, but feel free
to drop boilerplate and reorganize.

Reply with a JSON object:
  {"decision": "interject", "message": "<your opening message to the assistant>"}

Problem:
---
<system-instruction>
To do immediately, before using any skills or any other tools (one time only):

Rename the branch using `git branch -m`. Do not check the current branch name.

Choosing a branch name: use concrete, specific language, avoid abstract nouns, and be concise (<30 characters). Use the prefix "marcus-sa/" before the name.

</system-instruction>


add traces and policies to index.html and README.md

Context

We introduced a trace table (migration 0023) as a graph-native, hierarchical call tree for forensic tracing. Currently intent.trace_id points to record<trace>, enabling graph traversal from intents to their originating execution context.

However, the older subagent_traces field on message still stores execution traces as embedded arrays — denormalized, not independently queryable, and invisible to graph traversal.

Proposal

Migrate subagent_traces data from embedded array<object> on message into normalized trace recor
...[truncated]
---

Choosing a branch name: use concrete, specific language, avoid abstract nouns, and be concise (<30 characters). Use the prefix "marcus-sa/" before the name.

</system-instruction>


add traces and policies to index.html and README.md

Context

We introduced a trace table (migration 0023) as a graph-native, hierarchical call tree for forensic tracing. Currently intent.trace_id points to record<trace>, enabling graph traversal from intents to their originating execution context.

However, the older subagent_traces field on message still stores execution traces as embedded arrays — denormalized, not independently queryable, and invisible to graph traversal.

Proposal

Migrate subagent_traces data from embedded array<object> on message into normalized trace records:

A subagent invocation becomes a root trace record (type: "subagent_spawn")
Each step (tool call, message, text) becomes a child trace with parent_trace pointing to the root
The message record lin
...[truncated]"}

tool/observation that came back.

[tool: Read] file: /Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/kyiv-v1/.context/attachments/pasted_text_2026-03-11_00-29-36.txt {"file_path": "/Users/marcus/conductor/workspaces/brain-v1/kyiv-v1/.context/attachments/pasted_text_2026-03-11_00-29-36.txt"}

OBSERVATION:
---
1→Absolutely. If the VentureBeat article is right—that the "Process Layer" is the missing link—then the Policy Node is the most important piece of code you’ll write this week.
     2→
     3→Without a Policy Node, your Authorizer Agent is just "vibing" based on its training data. With a Policy Node, the Authorizer has a Legal Constitution to enforce.
     4→
     5→The Policy Node: The "Business Constitution" Spec
     6→
     7→In SurrealDB, we don't want a flat text file. We want a Graph-Based SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) that an agent can traverse like a map.
     8→
     9→1. The Schema (SurrealQL)
    10→
    11→SQL
    12→DEFINE TABLE policy SCHEMAFULL;
    13→
    14→-- The Core Identification
    15→DEFINE FIELD title ON policy TYPE string;
    16→DEFINE FIELD version ON policy TYPE string; -- e.g. "2026.03.10"
    17→DEFINE FIELD status ON policy TYPE string ASSERT $value IN ['active', 'draft', 'deprecated'];
    18→
    19→-- The Logic: Pre-conditions and Constraints
    20→DEFINE FIELD scope ON policy TYPE array; -- e.g. ["finance", "hiring", "api_access"]
    21→DEFINE FIELD rules ON policy TYPE array; 
    22→/* Example Rule Object:
    23→{
    24→  id: "max_spend_limit",
    25→  condition: "intent.budget_limit.amount <= 500",
    26→  effect: "permit",
    27→  priority: 1
    28→}
    29→*/
    30→
    31→-- The "Veto" Configuration
    32→DEFINE FIELD requires_human_approval ON policy TYPE bool DEFAULT 
...[truncated]
---

OBSERVATION:
---
1→# Brain
     2→
     3→**The operating system for autonomous organizations.**
     4→
     5→Your agents have amnesia. They can't talk to each other. You spend 80% of your time as a high-paid secretary, copy-pasting context between them. Brain is the self-correcting knowledge graph that gives your agents shared memory, governed autonomy, and verifiable intent.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## The Problem
    10→
    11→You are the integration layer between your AI agents.
    12→
    13→You already use AI agents. Your coding agent writes your code. Your chat assistant helps you think through architecture. Your editor agent autocompletes. But none of them share context.
    14→
    15→- Copy error logs from CI into your coding agent
    16→- Relay architecture decisions from a chat into your codebase
    17→- Manually track what was decided, what's blocked, what changed
    18→- Re-explain project state every time you start a new session
    19→- Context-switch between tools, losing continuity at every step
    20→
    21→Every agent you add makes it worse. Brain fixes this — not by replacing your agents, but by giving them shared memory.
    22→
    23→## Architecture
    24→
    25→Why a graph, not a message bus. Most platforms try "agent swarms" — agents messaging agents. That creates a game of telephone where instructions get distorted. The graph is the single source of truth.
    26→
    27→```text
    28→Human Layer
...[truncated]
---

From walled garden to

Governance telemetry

\n

Every decision is a node with a UUID, author, timestamp, and reasoning. Researchers and auditors can query the graph directly \u2014 no digging through chat logs or stdout dumps.

\n
\n
\n

Signed intent chains

\n

When an agent spends money or merges code, the graph records which intent authorized it, which authority scope permitted it, and which human (or consensus) approved it. The full chain is machine-readable.

\n
\n
\n

Eval-ready metrics

\n

Context retention, conflict resolution rate, authority distribution, decision velocity \u2014 all computable directly from the graph. No custom instrumentation required.

\n
\n
\n

The \"Judge\" pattern

\n

High-stakes actions go through an Authorizer Agent \u2014 a separate, privileged instance that validates intents against policy constraints before minting scoped tokens. The worker never sees master keys.

565:

✦ Suggestions

585:

⬢ Intents

586:

Every agent action starts as an intent — a structured request in the graph. Intents carry the full authorization context: what, why, and with what constraints. They're evaluated against authority scopes before execution.

589:

🧠 Learnings

590:

Agents that repeat mistakes are useless. Learnings are behavioral rules injected into agent prompts at runtime — created by humans directly or suggested by other agents. The system gets smarter as it works, not dumber.

776:
◈
Intents as Authorization ContextAgent actions start as intents in the graph. The authorization server evaluates intents against authority scopes, then mints a token scoped to exactly that action.
